emotional_core: The exhaustion of chasing perfect systems — and the quiet relief of returning to habits honest enough to survive your worst days.
visual_metaphor: A cluttered desk slowly clearing — not by adding a better organizer, but by removing everything except what's used daily.
key_quotes: Productivity systems fail because they ask you to maintain a perfect structure across an imperfect life.
The most productive people I know don't have complicated systems. They have a handful of small habits they do.
The number doesn't have to be impressive. It just has to be real.

story_frames.visual: Person surrounded by open Notion tabs, color-coded planners, and stacked productivity books — overwhelmed expression, laptop glow in dim room, visual chaos
Same desk, cleared — single open notebook, one pen, one coffee cup, morning light — calm and deliberate
Hand deliberately closing a laptop at a warm-lit desk, notebook open beside it with a few handwritten lines — the physical act of stopping
Person writing rapidly on a blank page — not structured notes, just fast and loose — visible relief in posture, pen moving freely
story_frames.text: Empty the head. Not organize it.

story_frames.visual: Close-up of a notebook page with 'DONE TODAY:' at the top and five lines filled in by hand — warm desk lamp, quiet evening atmosphere
Phone screen showing a 90-minute timer, then phone placed face-down deliberately on the desk beside a closed laptop — a small act of commitment
Person at desk in early morning, coffee steam rising, opening a notebook already filled with tomorrow's priorities — no hesitation, immediate start
